questions.title: Git library choice
questions.context: PROJECT.md locks 'zero external runtime deps'. Repo currently has no runtime deps at all. Phase 3 needs clone, branch, checkout, status, apply, reset. apps/factory-cli/src/main.test.ts:2 already uses node:child_process spawn for tests — so subprocess-to-git is already in use somewhere.

options.label: Shell out to git via node:child_process
options.description: Zero deps. Direct, predictable, matches dark-factory minimalism. Argv-only invocation lines up with REPO-04's no-shell-strings requirement. We own arg-array discipline. Tradeoff: we re-implement small bits of git ergonomics (porcelain parsing, ref formatting) ourselves.

options.label: isomorphic-git (pure JS)
options.description: Single npm dep, no native bindings, runs anywhere Node runs. Programmatic API for clone/branch/apply. Tradeoff: breaks the zero-deps lock; patch-apply / merge corner cases historically lag git proper; bundle weight.

options.label: simple-git (wrapper around git CLI)
options.description: Thin wrapper around the system git binary — same trust boundary as option (a) but with ergonomics. Tradeoff: still a runtime dep; still requires system git installed; we lose direct control of the argv that gets executed (matters for REPO-04).

questions.title: Workspace storage location on disk
questions.context: Each run gets a runId; Phase 1 already writes to `.protostar/runs/{id}/`. Workspace clones could live alongside, in a sibling tree, or in the OS temp dir.

options.label: .protostar/workspaces/{runId}/
options.description: Symmetric with .protostar/runs/. Easy to inspect post-mortem. Tradeoff: pollutes the project dir with full-repo clones; CI/dev disk usage grows fast; `.gitignore` must cover it.

options.label: OS temp dir (os.tmpdir()/protostar/{runId})
options.description: Clean. OS handles cleanup. No project-dir pollution. Tradeoff: harder to inspect after a failed run unless we copy artifacts back; tmp paths are platform-variable.

options.label: Configurable — `workspaceRoot` in repo-policy.json with `.protostar/workspaces/` default
options.description: Operator picks per-machine. Default keeps it discoverable. Tradeoff: one more knob; need to validate the path is outside the source repo to avoid recursive clones.

questions.title: Workspace lifecycle — fresh clone per run vs. cached
questions.context: Phase 10 will run ≥10 consecutive runs against the same sibling toy repo. Cloning every time is slow but safe; reusing is fast but invites cross-run contamination.

options.label: Fresh clone per run (no reuse)
options.description: Bulletproof isolation — Phase 5/6 review loops can't be poisoned by prior runs. Tradeoff: every run pays the clone cost; for dogfood loop with a small toy repo this is cheap, for larger targets it isn't.

options.label: Reusable workspace pool keyed by repo URL + base SHA
options.description: Reset-and-reuse: `git clean -fdx && git checkout {baseSHA}` between runs. Faster after warm-up. Tradeoff: contamination risk if reset is incomplete; complexity in pool management; harder to reason about Phase 5/6 outcomes.

options.label: Fresh clone per run for v1, leave pool door open via config
options.description: Default to (a). `repo-policy.json` reserves a `workspacePool: { enabled: false }` field that Phase 10+ can flip. Locks safe v1 behavior; defers optimization.

questions.title: Clone authentication mechanism
questions.context: Phase 7 will need GitHub PAT for delivery; Phase 3 needs clone auth. .env.example (REPO-09) gates this. Options range from least-invasive (system credential helper) to most-explicit (env var + repo-policy reference).

options.label: Defer to system git credential helper / SSH agent
options.description: Zero new surface. Operator already has git auth working on their machine. Tradeoff: invisible to admission decisions — we can't show evidence that 'we used credential X' in the run artifact.

options.label: Env-var token referenced by `RepoTarget.credentialRef`
options.description: REPO-01 explicitly says 'URL + credential ref'. credentialRef is a name (e.g. `GITHUB_PAT`); .env.example documents it; we read process.env at clone time. Captured in admission decision as 'credentialRef used: GITHUB_PAT' (never the value). Tradeoff: env-var sprawl as we add more remotes.

options.label: Both — credentialRef preferred; fall back to system helper for unset refs
options.description: Tries explicit first, system second. Tradeoff: two code paths; harder to reason about which actually authenticated.

questions.title: File-path cap enforcement shape
questions.context: Phase 2 Q-05 locked AuthorizedWorkspaceOp brand (capability envelope produces a brand; only branded ops can mutate). Phase 3 wires the actual fs calls. Question is: where does path-resolution + cap-check happen?

options.label: Single repo-owned FS adapter; all reads/writes go through it; check at entry
options.description: `packages/repo` exports a FsAdapter type with `readFile(op: AuthorizedWorkspaceOp, path)` etc. Single funnel; canonicalize + verify path is within workspace root + envelope allows it. Tradeoff: every package that touches files imports the adapter — invasive.

options.label: Mint-time cap-check; fs calls trust the brand
options.description: AuthorizedWorkspaceOp is minted only after path is canonicalized + envelope-checked. Once you have the brand, you can use plain fs. Mirrors Phase 2 ConfirmedIntent posture. Tradeoff: relies on brand discipline — a leaked brand is a leaked cap.

options.label: Both — adapter takes the brand and double-checks at entry
options.description: Belt-and-suspenders. Mint-time check in authority kernel; second canonicalize+verify inside the adapter. Catches stale brands and time-of-check/time-of-use races on resolved paths. Tradeoff: most ceremony.

questions.title: Symlink handling inside workspace
questions.context: An attacker-controlled repo could ship a symlink that points outside the workspace. Phase 3 has to decide before any read/write lands.

options.label: Refuse all symlinks inside workspace
options.description: Strictest. `lstat` every path; if symlink, refuse and emit a refusal artifact. Tradeoff: breaks legitimate symlinks (pnpm node_modules, etc.) — though node_modules lives outside the change-set in our model.

options.label: Resolve symlinks and re-verify target is inside workspace
options.description: Symlinks allowed iff target resolves inside the workspace root. Tradeoff: TOCTOU risk between resolve and use; complex when targets are themselves symlinks.

options.label: Refuse symlinks in change-set paths, allow in workspace tree at large
options.description: We refuse to *write* through a symlink; reads of pre-existing symlinks (node_modules) are fine. Tradeoff: split-brain rule; needs clear per-op semantics.

questions.title: Subprocess allowlist source of truth
questions.context: REPO-04 says 'allowlist + arg validation, never shell strings'. The allowlist could live in code, in repo-policy.json, or be derived from capability envelope.

options.label: Hardcoded baseline in `@protostar/repo` (git, pnpm, node, tsc)
options.description: Fixed, explicit, code-reviewable. Tradeoff: every new tool needs a code change; repos with custom build tools (cargo, make) need allowlist edits.

options.label: `.protostar/repo-policy.json` carries `commandAllowlist: [...]`
options.description: Per-target-repo configurable. Keeps the dark factory honest about what each target needs. Tradeoff: one more thing the operator has to maintain; risk of overly-broad allowlists.

options.label: Hardcoded baseline + repo-policy can extend (not override)
options.description: Baseline is always allowed (git, pnpm, node, tsc); repo-policy can add (cargo, make) but cannot remove. Tradeoff: 'cannot remove' may not always be correct (some repos may want to forbid pnpm).

questions.title: Subprocess argv validation rules
questions.context: Even with an allowlist, `git checkout $branch` lets attacker-controlled branch names inject flags (`--upload-pack=...`). REPO-04 must validate args, not just the binary.

options.label: Per-command argv schema (git: subset of subcommands + flag whitelist; pnpm: same)
options.description: Each allowlisted command has a schema in `@protostar/repo`: allowed subcommands, allowed flags, value pattern (e.g. branch names match `[a-zA-Z0-9._/-]+`). Tradeoff: maintenance — every new git operation needs schema updates.

options.label: Pattern-based — refuse args starting with `-` unless whitelisted; quote refs
options.description: Lighter rule: any arg starting with `-` is a flag and must be whitelisted; ref-like args validated against a regex; `--` separator forced before user-controlled values. Tradeoff: less precise; surprising failures.

options.label: Both — pattern guard at outer layer, per-command schema at inner layer
options.description: Defense in depth. Pattern guard catches obvious flag injection; per-command schema validates the rest. Tradeoff: most ceremony for v1.

questions.title: Subprocess stdout/stderr capture policy
questions.context: Phase 4 will spawn the LM Studio coder; Phase 3 sets the capture contract. Buffers can blow memory; streaming must be journalled; size caps must be admission-decision-evidenced.

options.label: Buffer with hard size cap (e.g. 5 MB stdout, 1 MB stderr); refuse beyond
options.description: Predictable, simple, capture-once. Beyond cap → AuthorizedSubprocessOp budget violation → refusal artifact. Tradeoff: legitimate large outputs (verify:full test logs) might bump the cap.

options.label: Stream to file in run dir; no in-memory buffer
options.description: `runs/{id}/subprocess/{n}-stdout.log`. Unlimited size, but bounded by disk. Phase 5 review reads from disk. Tradeoff: more I/O; harder to inline-evidence in the admission decision.

options.label: Stream to file + tail the last N KB into the admission decision evidence
options.description: Best of both. Full log on disk; admission-decision carries the tail. Tradeoff: most code; tail may not contain the relevant failure cause.

questions.title: Patch format / change-set representation
questions.context: Phase 4 will produce diffs from the LM Studio coder. `RepoChangeSet` currently has `patches: PatchArtifact[]` with `add|modify|delete` semantics — abstract, no payload. Phase 3 has to decide what the payload actually looks like.

options.label: Unified diff text; apply via `git apply --index --3way`
options.description: Standard format; matches what LLM coders emit naturally; git handles conflict resolution. Tradeoff: text-diff fragility (line numbers drift if base changes mid-run); 3way-merge surprises.

options.label: Filewise full-content payloads (`{path, op, contents}`)
options.description: Each patch carries the full new file contents; we write directly. Trivially reversible (we keep the original). Tradeoff: bloated payloads for large files; loses any notion of 'this is a 3-line change'.

options.label: Unified diff + per-file pre-image hash; apply via `git apply` with hash check
options.description: Diff is the payload but we capture a SHA-256 of each pre-image file; refuse to apply if pre-image doesn't match (catches concurrent-mutation, drift). Tradeoff: more ceremony; needs hash capture before patch generation in Phase 4.

questions.title: Atomicity / rollback mechanism
questions.context: REPO-05 says 'applyChangeSet is atomic; failure restores prior worktree state'. Mechanisms range from git-native (stash/reset) to OS-level (filesystem snapshot) to in-process (in-memory pre-image).

options.label: Pre-snapshot via `git stash --include-untracked` + apply + on-failure stash-pop
options.description: Git-native. Survives crashes (stash is durable). Reverts deletions and additions. Tradeoff: stash is shared global state; concurrent runs in the same workspace would conflict (mitigated by Q-03 fresh-clone-per-run).

options.label: Pre-snapshot tree to a tag/commit on a throwaway branch; reset on failure
options.description: Create `protostar/snapshot-{runId}` tag at clone-time; on failure `git reset --hard` to it. Tradeoff: pollutes refs in the workspace; meaningless if Q-03 is fresh-clone-per-run (we'd just re-clone).

options.label: Trust Q-03 fresh-clone-per-run as the rollback; just delete the workspace on failure
options.description: If every run is a fresh clone (Q-03 = a or c), 'rollback' is `rm -rf .protostar/workspaces/{runId}`. Atomic by definition. Tradeoff: only works if Q-03 lands on fresh-clone; doesn't generalize to pooled workspaces.

questions.title: Rollback granularity — per-file or whole change-set
questions.context: When patch 3 of 5 fails, do we roll back patches 1-2 too, or commit them and report partial?

options.label: All-or-nothing — any patch failure rolls back the entire change-set
options.description: Predictable. Phase 5/6 always sees a clean 'applied' or 'unchanged' worktree. Tradeoff: a 4-of-5 successful change can't be salvaged.

options.label: Best-effort — apply what works, report failures as evidence
options.description: Faster forward progress for the repair loop. Tradeoff: harder to reason about state; review pile sees partial diffs.

options.label: All-or-nothing in v1; reserve `mode: 'best-effort'` flag in RepoChangeSet for future
options.description: Default safe, leave the door open. Tradeoff: minor schema bloat for an unused field.

questions.title: Dirty-worktree detection method
questions.context: REPO-06 says 'refuses to operate on uncommitted changes unless explicitly allowed by capability envelope'. Question is what counts as 'dirty'.

options.label: `git status --porcelain` non-empty = dirty
options.description: Standard, covers staged, unstaged, untracked. Tradeoff: untracked files (e.g. .DS_Store) would block runs; needs a sensible default ignore.

options.label: `git status --porcelain --untracked-files=no` (ignore untracked)
options.description: Only fails on tracked-file modifications. More forgiving for fresh clones with stray files. Tradeoff: untracked malicious files survive — but Q-03 fresh-clone makes this moot.

options.label: Strict tracked + selective untracked allowlist (.protostar/, node_modules)
options.description: Tracked-dirty = block; untracked-dirty = block unless inside allowlisted paths. Tradeoff: most code; debatable allowlist contents.

questions.title: Capability envelope flag for dirty-worktree override
questions.context: REPO-06 reserves a 'capability envelope explicitly allows it' escape hatch. Where does that flag live in the capability envelope schema and what is it called?

options.label: `capabilityEnvelope.workspace.allowDirty: boolean` (default false)
options.description: New field on the workspace cap. Plain. Tradeoff: requires an additive bump to confirmedIntent schema (already at 1.1.0 from Phase 2 Q-18); could be a 1.2.0.

options.label: Reuse the existing `workspaceMode` enum: add `'dirty-allowed'`
options.description: Existing field gets a new variant. Tradeoff: enum widening — still a schema bump; couples 'dirty' to whatever `workspaceMode` already means.

options.label: No envelope flag — block always, log refusal; operator uses `--force-dirty` CLI
options.description: Keeps the schema clean. CLI flag is operator-facing only and never threads into ConfirmedIntent. Tradeoff: violates REPO-06's literal 'capability envelope explicitly allows' wording.

questions.title: workspaceRoot resolution helper home
questions.context: REPO-07: walk up to nearest `pnpm-workspace.yaml`. Helper has to live somewhere — and CONCERNS.md flagged the current `INIT_CWD ?? cwd()` as broken at apps/factory-cli/src/main.ts:150.

options.label: `@protostar/repo` exports `resolveWorkspaceRoot()`; CLI imports it
options.description: Domain-correct (repo owns repo-related path resolution). Tradeoff: factory-cli now depends on @protostar/repo for a previously-no-dep path resolution.

options.label: `apps/factory-cli/src/workspace-root.ts` keeps it; @protostar/repo doesn't expose it
options.description: Closest to current code. Tradeoff: AGENTS.md says 'side effects belong behind repo' — this is filesystem traversal, technically belongs in repo.

options.label: New tiny `@protostar/paths` package
options.description: Pure utility; no FS owner. Tradeoff: contradicts AGENTS.md 'avoid generic utils packages'.

questions.title: @dogpile/sdk resolution strategy
questions.context: Phase 1 already shipped `packages/dogpile-types` shim covering the contract surface (AgentSpec, DogpileOptions, budget, convergence, firstOf). REPO-08 asks for a fresh-clone-installable answer.

options.label: Expand `packages/dogpile-types` shim to cover the runtime surface; remove `link:` entirely
options.description: We already own the contract; vendor the runtime too. Tradeoff: we maintain the dogpile runtime in this repo until the public package exists; keeps zero external runtime deps.

options.label: Publish `@dogpile/sdk` to npm + pin a version
options.description: Cleanest long-term. Tradeoff: requires the dogpile sibling repo to ship a release; cross-repo coordination; introduces a runtime dep.

options.label: Pinned tarball under `vendor/` + `file:` link
options.description: No npm publish needed; reproducible installs. Tradeoff: tarball updates are manual; binary blob in git.

questions.title: .env.example scope — Phase 3 only or full Phase 4–7 forward-look
questions.context: REPO-09: '.env.example documents every env var the factory will read in Phases 4–7'. Question is whether to enumerate now or only the vars Phase 3 itself reads.

options.label: Phase 3 only (clone auth: e.g. GITHUB_PAT)
options.description: Conservative. We only document what we use. Tradeoff: violates REPO-09's literal 'Phases 4–7' wording; defers .env churn to those phases.

options.label: Forward-look: GITHUB_PAT, LM_STUDIO_ENDPOINT, LM_STUDIO_CODER_MODEL, LM_STUDIO_JUDGE_MODEL, RESEND_API_KEY (where known)
options.description: Matches REPO-09 literally. We commit to the env-var names now and downstream phases just read them. Tradeoff: names may drift; we may add some we don't end up using.

options.label: Forward-look + a 'reserved-not-yet-used' section commented out
options.description: Active section for what Phase 3 reads; commented-out `# Reserved for Phase N` block for forward-look. Tradeoff: most maintenance; commented-out env vars rot.

questions.title: In-process sacrificial repo for Phase 3 tests
questions.context: Success criterion: 'Tests against an in-process sacrificial git repo prove: clone, branch, file-write within caps, patch apply, rollback, dirty-worktree refusal'. Question is what 'sacrificial' means in practice.

options.label: Per-test ephemeral `git init` in os.tmpdir() with seeded commit
options.description: Each test sets up its own bare/working repo via subprocess git. Fully isolated. Tradeoff: every test pays the init cost; needs careful cleanup.

options.label: Fixture tarball checked into `__fixtures__/sacrificial-repo.tar.gz`; extracted per test
options.description: Predictable starting state; reproducible across machines. Tradeoff: binary in git; extract step still needs cleanup.

options.label: Test helper in `packages/repo/internal/test-fixtures` that builds the repo programmatically
options.description: Code-defined fixture: `buildSacrificialRepo({ commits, branches, dirtyFiles })` returns a path. Mirrors the brand-witness internal-subpath pattern from Phase 1/2. Tradeoff: test helper code to maintain; but most flexible for the per-criterion variations.
